text: Electrocatalysis provides a transformative pathway for the localized, on-demand production of industrial chemicals using intermittent renewable energy, enhancing grid resilience while streamlining supply logistics. In this work, we demonstrate the efficacy of a molecular copper boron-imidazolate cage, BIF-29(Cu), as a catalyst for the co-reduction of CO₂ and NO₃⁻ to produce urea. This system achieves a high selectivity of 68.5% and a current density of 424 μA cm⁻², establishing BIF-29(Cu) as one of the most selective catalysts identified to date for this complex C–N coupling reaction. Notably, this performance is achieved despite the catalyst possessing isolated, single-metal active sites. Utilizing a combination of electrochemical measurements, in situ spectroscopy, and atomic-scale simulations, we investigated the underlying Reaction mechanism. Our findings reveal that CO₂ and NO₃⁻ reduction reactions occur in tandem at distinct copper sites, with the C–N bond formation proceeding via the condensation of *CO and NH₂OH intermediates. This study underscores the potential of supramolecular metal–organic cages with precision-engineered, discrete active sites to facilitate highly efficient multi-reactant coupling processes.
